text: The surgeon identifies a 2.1 by 1.8 cm hypermetabolic lesion in the left rectus musculature, confirmed as adenocarcinoma. The patient is positioned supine for robotic-assisted resection. An optical trocar is placed in the left upper quadrant, with two additional trocars in the supraumbilical region and right lower quadrant. The abdominal wall metastasis is visible at the medial portion of the previous colostomy closure site. Monopolar scissors score the mass, which is excised circumferentially using traction and counter traction. Bipolar grasp elevates the abdomen for anterior dissection, ensuring correct plane and hemostasis. The mass is removed entirely, extracted through a small skin incision at the colostomy closure site using a ring forceps. A remnant of the mass is identified and excised to ensure complete resection.

The surgeon closes the defect in the abdominal wall using a 2-0 barbed suture in a continuous running fashion, taking evenly spaced bites on either side. After reaching the end of the defect, the suture is tightened and the needle is cut off. A second 2-0 barbed suture is used to complete a two-layered closure, ensuring airtight closure and good approximation of the fascial layers. Both needles are extracted through the skin incision site, and the skin incisions are closed with a running monofilament suture, concluding the procedure.
